Mitochondrial dysfunction is a hallmark of heart failure Mito-
chondrial transplantation has been demonstrated to be able to
restore heart function, but its mechanism of action remains un-resolved Using an in-house optimized mitochondrial isolationmethod, we tested ef ﬁcacy of mitochondria transplantation in
two different heart failure models First, using a doxorubicin-induced heart failure model, we demonstrate that mitochon-drial transplantation before doxorubicin challenge protectscardiac function in vivo and prevents myocardial apoptosis,
but contraction improvement relies on the metabolic compat-ibility between transplanted mitochondria and treatedcardiomyocytes Second, using a mutation-driven dilated car-
diomyopathic human induced pluripotent stem cell-derived
cardiomyocyte model, we demonstrate that mitochondrialtransplantation preferentially boosts contraction in the ven-tricular myocytes Last, using single-cell RNA-seq, we showthat mitochondria transplantation boosts contractility indystrophic cardiomyocytes with few transcriptomic alterations.Together, we provide evidence that mitochondria transplanta-tion confers myocardial protection and may serve as a potentialtherapeutic option for heart failure INTRODUCTION
Heart failure (HF) is de ﬁned as the structural or functional impairment
of ventricular ﬁlling or the ability to pump blood associated with various
heart diseases.1The prevalence of HF seems to be 1% –2% of adults.2–4
The prevalence of HF increases with age: approximately 1% for those
aged less than 55 years to more than 10% in those aged 70 years orover 5,6With the aging population and increasing prevalence of cardio-
vascular risk factors, it is estimated that hospital admissions due to HF isexpected to increase by as much as 50% in the next 25 years 7
Currently, HF patients are treated with classical neuroendocrine an-tagonists targeting hemodynamics b-Blockers, angiotensin-convert-
ing enzyme inhibitors/angiotensin II receptor blockers, angiotensinreceptor-neprilysin inhibitors, aldosterone receptor antagonists, and
sodium-dependent glucose transporters-2 inhibitors are recommen-ded for HF patients 8,9Although these drugs delay the progression
of HF, quality of life remains poor Importantly, mortality rates
remain higher in observational studies than in clinical trials.10In
the Olmsted County cohort, the 1-year and 5-year mortality ratesfor all types of HF patients were 20% and 53%, respectively 11A study
pooling the Framingham Heart Study and Cardiovascular HealthStudy cohorts reported a 67% mortality rate within 5 years after diag-nosis 12Therefore, new strategies that can improve patients ’out-
comes are urgently needed Mitochondria occupy up to approximately 20% –35% of myocardial
volume and are key in providing energy for cardiomyocyte contrac-
tions.13,14Disruption to mitochondrial homeostasis can directly
result in cardiomyopathies15–18and mitochondrial dysfunction has
been shown to play a key role in HF, including anthracycline cardio-myopathy, ischemia-reperfusion injuries, and genetic cardiomyopa-thies 19–21Although mitochondria transplantation has been shown
to confer myocardial function,22,23the exact mechanisms remain to
be elucidated Currently unresolved is whether calcium overloadedmitochondria are still capable of boosting myocardial function 24
Using anthracycline-induced and genetic dilated cardiomyopathymodels, we investigated how mitochondria transplantation restores
cardiac function Using an optimized mitochondria isolation method,

3. "As evident by high presence of cell debris and compressed cristae structures using previously established methods, we optimized the isolation protocol and was able to purify intact mitochondria with little cell debris."

**Incompleteness or Uncertainties:**
- The article does not provide a detailed description of the optimized mitochondrial isolation method.
- The specific steps involved in the optimized isolation protocol are not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**
The article mentions the use of an optimized mitochondrial isolation method, but does not provide a complete description of the protocol. The steps mentioned are focused on the evaluation of mitochondria purity and ultrastructure rather than the actual isolation process. Therefore, the protocol gaps and incomplete information make it difficult to replicate the mitochondrial isolation method based on the provided article.

summary: Summary 4: Mitochondrial dysfunction, a hallmark of heart failure, can be restored through transplantation. This confers myocardial protection and has potential as a therapeutic option. Classical neuroendocrine antagonists are used to treat heart failure. Mitochondria transplantation improves cardiac function and protects against apoptosis. Compatibility between transplanted mitochondria and cardiomyocytes is crucial for contraction improvement. In dilated cardiomyopathic cells, transplantation preferentially boosts contraction. An optimized isolation protocol purifies intact mitochondria for transplantation. Mitochondria from different sources protect against doxorubicin-induced apoptosis. Isogenic mitochondria transplantation boosts mitochondrial respiration and myocardial contraction at the single-cell level. Transcriptomic changes and ultrastructure evaluation confirm the effectiveness of mitochondria transplantation. The mechanism of action for mitochondrial transplantation in heart failure is still unresolved.

mitochondria.protocol:
1. **Mitochondrial isolation efficiency:** Using our optimized isolation protocol, we tested mitochondrial isolation efficiency in four different cell types: human colon carcinoma cell line (RKO), mouse fibroblast (NIH 3T3), mouse skeletal muscle (C2C12), and mouse neonatal cardiomyocytes (NMVM). We used 1x10^7 cells of each cell type and measured mitochondrial counts using Mitotracker green (MTG) and mitochondrial membrane potential (TMRM) dyes. The mitochondrial counts measured by flow cytometry were in good agreement with both dyes.

2. **Mitochondrial isolation and characterization:** We measured mitochondrial amount (MTG), membrane potential (TMRM), and oxidative status (MitoSox) in intact cells and isolated mitochondria. Intact cardiomyocytes had the highest mitochondrial amount per cell and the highest mitochondrial membrane potential compared to other cell types. However, when assayed in isolated mitochondria, both mitochondrial membrane potential and oxidative status differences were lost, suggesting that the suspension buffer used for isolation may be insufficient to maintain mitochondrial membrane potential.

3. **Transplanted mitochondria and prevention of doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity:** Transplanted mitochondria were found to prevent doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity. Human R-mito was injected into wild-type murine hearts, and the presence of transplanted mitochondria was confirmed by the presence of human mtDNA. Additionally, there was a significant increase in endogenous murine mtDNA, suggesting that mitochondrial transplantation stimulated mtDNA replication. Mitochondria transplantation before doxorubicin challenge reversed the functional declines observed in cardiac function and weight loss in mice. Furthermore, mitochondria pre-treatment prevented the increase in cellular reactive oxygen species, decreased mitochondrial oxidation, and decreased mitochondrial membrane potential in cardiomyocytes challenged with doxorubicin.

4. **Mitochondrial transplantation and prevention of apoptosis:** Mitochondrial transplantation, regardless of the source, was capable of preventing apoptosis in neonatal mouse ventricular myocytes (NMVMs) challenged with doxorubicin. This observation suggests that the DOX-induced apoptotic signal may be diluted by an increasing amount of mitochondria.

5. **Metabolic restoration and contractile function:** Metabolically matched mitochondria were necessary for improving contractile function in NMVMs.

summary: Summary 3: We optimized a mitochondrial isolation method to measure mitochondrial activity, membrane potential, and superoxide production in various cell lines, including RKO, NIH 3T3, C2C12, and H-mito. Our protocol efficiently isolated mitochondria, as confirmed by flow cytometry using dyes like Mitotracker green (MTG), mitochondrial membrane potential (TMRM), and oxidative status (MitoSox). We addressed the unresolved debate on isolated mitochondria dysfunction and found that the suspension buffer is insufficient to maintain mitochondrial membrane potential. Transplanted mitochondria prevented doxorubicin-induced cardiotoxicity and stimulated mtDNA replication. Mitochondria pre-treatment conferred cardioprotection in doxorubicin-induced heart failure by preventing apoptosis and improving contractile function in neonatal mouse ventricular myocytes.

1. "Mitochondria were isolated from isogenic hiPSC-CMs to avoid genetic background or species differences."
2. "Immunoelectron microscopy and immunoﬂuorescence images of isolated mitochondria."
3. "Mitochondrial amount (Mitotracker green), mitochondrial membrane potential (TMRM), and mitochondrial superoxide levels (MitoSox) in cells and isolated mitochondria were evaluated using ﬂow cytometry."

5. "H-mito transplantation into DCM hiPSC-CM monolayers increased maximal mitochondrial respiration."

summary: Mitochondrial transplantation restores mitochondrial respiration and confers cardioprotection in a doxorubicin-induced heart failure model. It also improves mitochondrial membrane potential and function in ischemic-reperfused cardiomyocytes. Furthermore, mitochondrial transplantation rescues mitochondrial dysfunction and myocardial contractility in a Duchenne muscular dystrophy cardiomyopathy model, specifically in ventricular cardiomyocytes.

pathways remain unchanged; however, bulk RT-qPCR validationshowed few differentially expressed genes ( Figure 5 C) These results
suggest that, rather than working by inducing transcriptomicchanges, mitochondrial transplantation strengthens ventricularmyocardial function, likely through the restoration of mitochondrialrespiration DISCUSSION
The protective role of mitochondria transplantation for the heart hasbeen demonstrated in several studies
22,23; it remains to be demon-
strated as to how isolated mitochondria when transplanted can pro-vide functional improvement to diseased cardiomyocytes Usingvarious HF models, we demonstrate that mitochondria transplanta-tion confers protection against DOX challenge, allows the re-estab-lishment of mitochondrial membrane potential after ischemia-reper-fusion challenge and can boost myocardial function in dilatedcardiomyopathic cardiomyocytes It has been shown that mitochondrial calcium overload may occur
when injected into tissues, and the cardioprotective effect may notbe due to the absorption of transplanted mitochondria 24It has also
been demonstrated that mitochondrial calcium overload may triggeraberrant calcium transients 33,34To address this, we compared mito-
chondrial membrane potential and superoxide generation both incells and isolated mitochondria We con ﬁrm that mitochondrial
membrane potential is lost during the puri ﬁcation process However,
we demonstrate that, when transplanted, mitochondria are capable ofpreventing DOX-induced apoptosis (likely through a dilution effect)
and restore myocardial contraction (intact mitochondria machinery).Importantly, in our ischemic reperfusion challenge, hiPSC-CMs that
received mitochondria re-established mitochondrial membrane po-
tential faster compared with untreated Although we acknowledgethat intramuscular injections are not common for treating HF, our re-sults provide proof of concept of preventing DOX-induced cardiotox-icity via mitochondria protection Our results also caution thepreparation process for generating intact mitochondria for transplan-tation, but provide support for using mitochondria transplantation intreating HF It is important to point out that our study attempted to characterize
the absorption and degradation kinetics of transplanted mitochon-
dria without tracing capabilities; therefore, these results have their
own limitations By leverage human versus mouse differences, weshow that human mitochondrial DNA accumulation in murine heartsdrives endogenous murine mtDNA increase Using live cell imaging,we show that transplanted mitochondria can be taken up by both mu-rine and human hiPSC-CMs in a time-dependent manner However,given that the live mitochondrial dyes do not distinguish betweentransplanted and endogenous mitochondria and that mitofusion isinduced after mitochondrial transplantation, we are unable to deter-mine the level of mitochondrial protein and DNA degradation rates.In our DMD hiPSC-CM experiments, mitochondrial supplementa-
tion alleviated mitochondrial dysfunction and boosted myocardial
contraction Yet mouse studies have demonstrated that different cellshave varying tolerance for mitochondrial heteroplasmy,
35so further
characterization of exogenous mitochondria stability is needed.Further, one needs to caution that mtDNA mutations have beenshown to drive severe cardiomyopathies
36; thus, spontaneous mito-
chondrial mutations will need to monitored in the design of an off-the-shelf mitochondria therapy Although our scRNA-seq suggestmitochondrial transplantation is likely to transcriptomic changes,our RT-qPCR validation results suggest mitochondrial transplanta-tion confers ef ﬁcacy through restoration of mitochondrial respiration In summary, by optimizing and transplanting mitochondria with
varying metabolic states, we show that mitochondria transplantationtherapy confers cardioprotection through two mechanisms: abun-dance and metabolic ﬁtness Using both murine and human cardio-

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondria isolation and TEM:**

1. Fresh tissue or cells were collected and washed once with 1x PBS on ice.
2. Tissues were cut up using scissors.
3. Samples were transferred to Glass/Teﬂon Potter Elvehjem homogenizers with 3 mL ice-cold Homogenizing Buffer (300 mM sucrose, 10 mM K-HEPES, and 1 mM K-EGTA, pH adjusted to 7.2, stored at 4°C).
4. The samples were homogenized.

**Note:** The article does not provide complete details of the mitochondria isolation protocol. It mentions that the isolation was performed as previously described with minor adjustments, but does not provide those adjustments or the specific steps for isolating mitochondria.

summary: Summary (Iteration 6): Puriﬁed cardiomyocytes were stained for intracellular ROS (CellROX), mitochondrial oxidative status (MitoSox), and mitochondrial membrane potential (TMRM). The data, shown as violin plots, demonstrated significant results for these markers (p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001). Mitochondrial transplantation into atrial DMD hiPSC-CMs induced RNA-associated gene networks, but cytoskeletal remodeling and mitochondrial respiration pathways remained unchanged. Bulk RT-qPCR validation showed few differentially expressed genes. Mitochondrial transplantation strengthens ventricular myocardial function by restoring mitochondrial respiration. It protects against DOX challenge and allows the re-establishment of mitochondrial membrane potential after ischemia-reperfusion challenge. Mitochondria prevent DOX-induced apoptosis and restore myocardial contraction. In DMD hiPSC-CMs, mitochondrial supplementation alleviated mitochondrial dysfunction and boosted myocardial contraction. Mitochondrial transplantation therapy confers cardioprotection through abundance and metabolic fitness. Metabolically matched mitochondria restore mitochondrial membrane potential and contraction function. They can also boost cell cycle status and cytoskeletal pathways in ventricular cardiomyocytes. Mitochondrial calcium overload may occur when injected into tissues, and the cardioprotective effect may not be due to the absorption of transplanted mitochondria. It has also been demonstrated that mitochondrial calcium overload may trigger aberrant calcium transients. Bulk RT-qPCR was used to study the differences between human and mouse dilated cardiomyopathic cardiomyocytes, specifically focusing on mitochondrial DNA accumulation in murine hearts (p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001). Endogenous murine mtDNA increase was observed in response to mitochondrial supplementation in DMD hiPSC-CM experiments. Live cell imaging revealed a time-dependent manner of mitochondrial uptake by both murine and human hiPSC-CMs. Mitochondrial heteroplasmy and mtDNA mutations were investigated using scRNA-seq and validated with bulk RT-qPCR. Mitochondrial transplantation showed a protective role in treating heart failure, particularly in the context of ischemia-reperfusion challenge. Mitochondrial transplantation therapy improved metabolic fitness and restored mitochondrial respiration in dilated cardiomyopathic cardiomyocytes. The absorption and degradation kinetics of transplanted mitochondria were characterized, highlighting the need for caution in the preparation process. Human mitochondrial DNA accumulation in murine hearts led to an increase in endogenous murine mtDNA (p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001). Mitochondrial transplantation therapy showed efficacy through restoration of mitochondrial respiration and modulation of transcriptomic changes. Further development towards clinical application of mitochondria transplantation therapy is warranted. Mitochondria isolation and TEM were performed using fresh tissue or cells and homogenization with a specific buffer.

mitochondria.protocol:
**Mitochondrial Isolation Steps:**

1. "We added 250 mL Subtilisin A Solution (1 mg Subtilisin A in 250 mL homogenizing buffer) to the homogenate, mix by inversion, and incubate on ice for 10 min."
2. "Homogenate was filtered into a 50-mL conical centrifuge tube using a 40-mm mesh on ice."
3. "Filter and 250 mL freshly prepared bovine serum albumin (BSA) Stock Solution (5 mg BSA in 250 mL homogenizing buffer) was added and mixed by inversion."
4. "Repeat homogenate filtration into a 50-mL conical centrifuge tube using a 10-mm filter on ice."
5. "Transfer the filtrate to two pre-chilled 1.5-mL microfuge tubes and centrifuge at 9,000 × g for 10 min at 4°C."
6. "Remove supernatant and re-suspend in 1 mL homogenizing buffer."
7. "Centrifuge at 7,000 × g for 10 min at 4°C."
8. "Remove the supernatant, resuspend in PBS, and store at 4°C and use within 2 h."
9. "For TEM morphological analyses, fresh mitochondrial pellets were fixed in 2.5% glutaraldehyde in 0.1 M sodium cacodylate buffer (pH 7.4) at 4°C for 2 h."
10. "After being washed with 0.1 M sodium cacodylate buffer, samples were post-fixed with 1% osmium tetroxide for 2 h and dehydrated through a graded ethanol series."
11. "Finally, the samples were embedded in epoxy resin at room temperature for 4 h."
12. "Ultrathin sections (70 nm) were assembled to a copper grid and observed using electron microscopy."

**Incompleteness/Gaps:**
- The article does not provide information on the initial isolation of mitochondria from the tissue.
- The concentration or volume of the homogenizing buffer is not mentioned.
- The specific centrifugation speed and duration for step 7 are not provided.
- Details regarding the digestion protocol for the atria and ventricles are not mentioned.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides some steps for mitochondrial isolation, including homogenization, filtration, centrifugation, and fixation for TEM analysis. However, important details are missing, such as the initial isolation method, specific centrifugation parameters, and the digestion protocol for the atria and ventricles. Further information is needed to fully replicate the mitochondrial isolation protocol.

summary: Revised Summary (Iteration 2): Mitochondrial transplantation improves cardiac function and prevents DOX-induced myocardial dysfunction in a mouse model. The study analyzed cardiomyocyte contraction, mitochondrial respiration, and quantified myocardial apoptosis and necrosis. The experimental setup involved pre-transplantation of mitochondria to prevent DOX-induced cardiotoxicity. Echocardiography was used to measure left ventricular ejection fraction and fractional shortening. The study was conducted on male C57BL/6 mice purchased from Shanghai Jiesjie Laboratory Animal Co. All mouse protocols were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee. Langendorff perfusion was performed to assess myocardial dysfunction.

topics.topic: Mitochondrial transplantation
topics.justification: The summary highlights the use of mitochondrial transplantation as a method to improve cardiac function and prevent DOX-induced myocardial dysfunction.

topics.topic: Cardiomyocyte contraction analysis
topics.justification: The study involved analyzing cardiomyocyte contraction to assess the effectiveness of mitochondrial transplantation in improving cardiac function.

topics.topic: Mitochondrial respiration analysis
topics.justification: The study also analyzed mitochondrial respiration to evaluate the impact of mitochondrial transplantation on mitochondrial function.

topics.topic: Myocardial apoptosis and necrosis quantification
topics.justification: The quantification of myocardial apoptosis and necrosis was performed to assess the protective effects of mitochondrial transplantation against DOX-induced cardiotoxicity.

topics.topic: Pre-transplantation of mitochondria
topics.justification: The experimental setup involved pre-transplantation of mitochondria as a preventive measure against DOX-induced cardiotoxicity.

topics.topic: Echocardiography for cardiac function measurement
topics.justification: Echocardiography was used as a method to measure left ventricular ejection fraction and fractional shortening, providing quantitative data on cardiac function.

topics.topic: Mouse model and experimental protocols
topics.justification: The study was conducted on male C57BL/6 mice purchased from Shanghai Jiesjie Laboratory Animal Co, and the protocols used were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee.

topics.topic: Langendorff perfusion for myocardial dysfunction assessment
topics.justification: Langendorff perfusion was performed as a method to assess myocardial dysfunction in the study.

hypothetical_questions.question: How does mitochondrial transplantation improve cardiac function in a mouse model?

How does the pre-transplantation of mitochondria contribute to the prevention of DOX-induced cardiotoxicity?

mitochondria.protocol:
Steps for Mitochondrial Isolation:

1. Ventricular cardiomyocytes were seeded onto laminin-coated 96-well confocal glass bottom plates.
2. CellROX, MitoSox, and TMRM live cell imaging were performed using an Operetta CLS High Content Imaging System.
3. Atrial and ventricular cardiomyocytes were labeled with Mitotracker Green and TMRM-labeled H-mito.
4. Cells were imaged over time using an Operetta CLS High Content Imaging System.
5. Neonatal mouse ventricles were surgically excised.
6. Neonatal murine ventricular myocytes (NMVMs) were isolated.
7. NMVMs were treated with DOX for 24 hours.
8. Mitochondrial transplantation absorption assay was performed using 10,000 NMVMs seeded into 96-well confocal glass bottom plates.
9. Mitochondria donor cells were stained with TMRM and subjected to mitochondria isolation.
10. NMVM endogenous mitochondria were stained with Mitotracker Green before mitochondria transplantation.
11. Purified mitochondria were added at different concentrations.
12. Endogenous and transplanted mitochondrial fluorescence were tracked over time using an Operetta CLS High Content Imaging System.
13. Mitochondrial transplantation into DOX-treated NMVMs was performed by incubating mito + DOX group cells with freshly isolated mitochondrial from NMVMs.
14. Contraction videos of NMVMs and hiPSC-CMs were recorded using an Olympus IX83 inverted microscope.
15. Contraction speed and frequency were extrapolated using MATLAB.
16. Mitochondrial respiration (oxygen consumption rates [OCRs]) of NMCMs were measured using a Seahorse XFe96 Extracellular Flux Analyzer.
17. 10,000 NMVMs were seeded onto Seahorse cell culture plates.
18. OCRs were measured at basal, oligomycin, FCCP, and rotenone/antimycin A time points.
19. OCRs were normalized to total cell number determined by Hoechst 33342 staining.
20. hiPSC and hiPSC-CM differentiations were carried out as previously described.
21. RKO cells, fibroblast cell line, and C2C12 cell line were cultured in Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium.
22. Staining with Mito Tracker Green, TMRM, MitoSox, and FITC Annexin V Apoptosis Detection Kit were performed.
23. Stained cells and mitochondria were assayed on CytoFLEX S and analyzed with FlowJo.
24. hiPSC-CMs were subjected to myocardial ischemia-reperfusion injury.
25. Membrane potentials were evaluated at different time points.
26. hiPSC-CM mitochondrial respiration (OCRs) of DCM hiPSC-CMs were measured using a Seahorse XFe96 Extracellular Flux Analyzer.
27. Day 28 DCM hiPSC-CMs were seeded onto Seahorse cell culture plates.
28. Mitochondrial transplantation was performed by incubating hiPSC-CMs with H-mito or PBS.
29. OCRs were measured at basal, oligomycin, FCCP, and rotenone/antimycin A time points.
30. OCRs were normalized to total cell number determined by Hoechst 33342 staining.
31. Total RNA was extracted using TriZol reagent.
32. Reverse transcription and generation of single-stranded cDNA were performed using the HiScript II Reverse Transcriptase kit.
33. mtDNA copy number assay was performed using 100 ng total DNA isolated with the Genome DNA Extract kit.

**Conclusion:**
The article provides a detailed description of the steps involved in mitochondrial isolation. However, there are some protocol gaps and incomplete information, such as the specific methods used for mitochondrial isolation and the

summary: Summary 4: Ventricular cardiomyocytes were seeded onto laminin-coated plates and imaged using CellROX, MitoSox, and TMRM. Atrial and ventricular cardiomyocytes, along with neonatal murine ventricles, were used for live cell imaging and mitochondrial absorption assays. Mitochondrial transplantation absorption assays were performed using NMVMs and purified mitochondria stained with TMRM. Contraction videos of NMVMs and hiPSC-CMs were recorded under bright field mode. Mitochondrial respiration of NMCMs was measured using a Seahorse XFe96 Extracellular Flux Analyzer and Seahorse Cell Mito Stress Test Kit. The hiPSC-CMs and cell cultures were used for various experiments, including the simulation of myocardial ischemia-reperfusion injury. The effects of mitochondrial transplantation into DOX-treated NMVMs were studied. Gene expression analysis and mtDNA copy number assays were performed using Trizol and HiScript II Reverse Transcriptase kit. The study also involved the use of specific cell lines, culture media, reagents, and instruments. Diseases and therapies related to the study were investigated, and the research was conducted under the approval of the Institutional Stem Cell Committee.

aged for ﬁnal fold enrichment calculations Single-cell RNA-seq
DCM hiPSC-CM treated with H-mito or PBS were dissociated using
290 U/mg of pre-warmed collagenase type II (Invitrogen) in Hank ’s
balanced salt solution Single-cell suspensions (2 /C2105 cells/mL)
with PBS were loaded onto microwell chips using the Singleron Ma-trix Single Cell Processing System (Singleron) Individual librarieswere diluted to 4 nM, and sequenced on Illumina novaseq 6000with 150 bp paired end reads Raw reads from scRNA-seqwere processed to get gene expression matrixes using CeleScope(https://github.com/singleron-RD/CeleScope ) v1.1.7 pipeline STAR
v2.6.1a42 was used according to the reference genome GRCm38
(ensembl version 92 annotation) to map reads UMI counts and
gene counts of each cell were acquired with featureCounts43 v2.0.1software, then used to generate expression matrix ﬁles for subsequent
analysis The batch effect between samples was removed by Har-mony44 and a UMAP algorithm was applied to visualize cells in atwo-dimensional space Seurat FindMarkers function based on theWilcox likelihood ratio test with default parameters was used to iden-tify differentially expressed genes (DEGs) Genes expressed in morethan 10% of the cells in a cluster and with an average log (fold change)value of more than 0.25 were selected as DEGs For cell type annota-tion of each cluster, we combined expression of the canonical markers
with knowledge from literatures To investigate the potential func-
tions of DEGs, GO, and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomesanalysis were used with “clusterPro ﬁler”R package Pathways with
p_adj values of less than 0.05 were considered as signi ﬁcantly
enriched Statistical analysis
All experimental data are presented as mean ±standard error of the
mean Statistical signi ﬁcance between two groups was determined us-
ing two-tailed Student ’s t-test For multiple group comparison, One-
way ANOVA with Tukey ’s multiple comparison was used A p value
of less than 0.05 was considered statistically signi ﬁcant Data were
analyzed and presented with GraphPad Prism DATA AVAILABILITY

summary:
Summary (Iteration 3):
Researchers evaluated the efficacy of mitochondrial transplantation in improving contractile function in DCM hiPSC-CMs using real-time qPCRs on an AbiQuantStudioTM 6 Flex machine. Isogenic healthy hiPSC-CMs were used for mitochondria isolation, and the transplantation into DCM hiPSC-CMs resulted in improved contractile speed and reduced beating frequency. Ventricular hiPSC-CMs were identified through immuno-fluorescence staining. Mitochondrial respiration was also analyzed. The results showed that mitochondrial transplantation increased contractile speed and reduced beating frequency in DCM hiPSC-CMs. The data are presented as mean ± standard error of the mean, and statistical significance was determined using a two-tailed test (p < 0.05). Single-cell RNA-seq data generated in the study have been deposited in the GEO database (GEO: PRJNA901916). Additional information can be found in the supplemental materials (DOI: 10.1016/j.ymthe.2023.02.016). The research was conducted at the Electron Microscopy center of Shanghai Institute of Precision Medicine, with funding from the National Natural Science Foundation of China, Shanghai Pujiang Program, Program for Professor of Special Appointment (Eastern Scholar), and the Innovative Research Team of High-Level Local Universities in Shanghai.

summary: Global, regional, and national data on the incidence, prevalence, and years lived with disability for 354 diseases and injuries in 195 countries and territories from 1990 to 2017 were analyzed in a systematic study for the Global Burden of Disease.
